Video Title: Why I Quit Working At An Amazon Warehouse Job
transcript:
In this video, I want to talk about Amazon—what it’s like to work at an Amazon warehouse, why I left, whether it’s for you, and what happens on your first day. This is my personal opinion, and I’ll be honest.
First day / orientation:
 On your first day, you go to orientation. They show you a presentation on a projector—basically a screen you stare at for an hour or two (maybe more). That’s considered training, but honestly you can’t really learn warehouse work just by staring at a screen for hours.
After orientation, they take you into the warehouse. I’m a blue-collar worker (welder/metal fabricator) and I’ve used tools my whole life, but warehouse work was a completely different world—and not in a good way. The videos they show are very animated and make the job look easier and nicer than it actually is.
When I walked into the warehouse, it felt robotic—like a machine-based operation where you’re expected to function like a machine. The work is repetitive and you’re on your feet for long hours.
Role and tasks (shipping/receiving):
 I worked overnight shifts (the only position available). The pay wasn’t good—around $18.50/hour (in 2023). It was full-time with benefits, so I took it while I was in welding school.
In shipping/receiving, you’re working around a large conveyor belt. You have a station/aisle and you scan packages and place them into assigned bins/locations. Sometimes you’re the person taking items off the conveyor belt and loading them into carts, and other people behind you scan and place them in the aisles.
That part was stressful because if you’re slow—even for a moment—packages pile up. Eventually the conveyor belt has to stop and someone has to come help you clear the backlog, then they turn it back on. It can get overwhelming fast.
Training and pace:
 There wasn’t much real training. They show you the basics and then you’re expected to perform quickly. The system feels designed to rush you and push you to move faster. Everything is time-based: trucks arrive on schedule, packages must be scanned and sorted quickly, and delays create more pressure. That’s why turnover is high.
Breaks and rules:
 Breaks were 45 minutes (overnight). On my first break I took a 30-minute nap because I was exhausted. People rely on energy drinks, but no matter what, fatigue catches up with you.
You have to be back from break exactly on time. I was told that if you’re even one minute late, they can deduct time from your paycheck (I heard something like 10 minutes deducted).
Bathroom breaks felt controlled. You often have to ask for permission, and managers act like they can’t stop the conveyor belt. The first time I asked, someone even asked how long I’d be gone and told me not to take more than 10 minutes.
Being monitored / micromanaged:
 One day I had to use the bathroom and I just left because I couldn’t find anyone to ask. When I came back, a manager approached with a computer and said it showed exactly how long I was gone (down to seconds). He even asked if I needed a doctor’s note. That’s when I realized how closely everything is monitored.
To me, you’re treated like a number. It felt like the military in that sense—just a number in the system.
Why I left / advice:
 After about a month, I couldn’t do it anymore. I was getting no sleep because I worked overnight and also had welding school at 5:00 PM. It caught up with my body and I felt mentally drained all the time.
If you’re thinking about working at an Amazon warehouse, I’d recommend reconsidering. Don’t do it just for the benefits—lots of jobs have benefits now. If you want to build skills, a warehouse job isn’t the best place for that. If you do take it, use it as a short-term stepping stone, not a long-term plan.
If you don’t like being micromanaged or watched constantly, it’s not the job for you. Pack your own food because vending machine food is expensive. Wear comfortable clothes, and get comfortable composite-toe boots if you can (they’re lighter).
That’s my experience. I would never work there again.

Video Title: What The Amazon Flex Overnight Shift Looks Like In The Hood
transcript:
It’s 2:00 in the morning. Have you ever woken up in the middle of the night and thought, “Damn, I want to work”? Me neither. But Amazon Flex has work for you. I picked up a 3:30 a.m. shift at Amazon—let’s go.
It’s cold. Honestly, I don’t even know why I’m up. I played tennis around noon, did chores, took a shower, went to sleep, and my schedule got messed up. I opened the Amazon Flex app and, of course, there are 3:00 a.m. shifts. I want to show you what the graveyard shift is like.
Arriving at the delivery station:
 I get there around 3:15. We’re in a garage with a lot of electric vehicles. One cool thing is that Amazon warehouses are all the same but different—each one has its own design. It’s 3:21 a.m. and I have a three-and-a-half-hour block.
I scan in and… 45 packages. Are you kidding me? I’m sorting them 1 through 45 so it’s easier to find each stop. In my Honda Civic, it’s packed. The itinerary is 41 stops, 45 packages, and we’re delivering in the hood.
There are two places you don’t want to be at 3:00 a.m.: the countryside (where you can’t see anything), or the hood—unfortunately, that’s where I’m going. First stop is eight minutes away.
Delivering at night:
 The stops are close together—less than five minutes, sometimes less than three. But delivering in the hood is stressful. There’s no parking, lots of one-way streets, and people are out on the streets. I’ve delivered here before and didn’t like it, but I’ve never done it at 3:00 a.m.
I’m skeptical about leaving my car running while I deliver—anyone could jump out. I have the key fob in my pocket, but it still feels risky.
At one building, I can’t get into the mailroom even with the code, so I leave the package by the door. I call it a half success, half fail. All I can do is hope it’s fine.
Some places are easier, like Amazon lockers. Overnight, one benefit is the streets are empty—during the day, these streets are packed. But you still have to be careful, especially if there’s police around.
Finishing the shift:
 By the time the sky starts turning brighter, it’s almost 6:00 and the sun is coming up. We’re almost done—only a few packages left.
That’s it. We finished the graveyard shift. It’s 6:30 a.m., and we completed the 3.5-hour block with about 30 minutes to spare. It paid $77 for around 40 stops. Would I do it again? Maybe—maybe in the summertime. It wasn’t too bad today since we were downtown, the nicer part of the area.
I had one package I couldn’t deliver because I couldn’t access the building, so I returned it to the Amazon hub. Now I’m going to spend a few minutes watching the sunrise.
Topic: Amazon Flex graveyard shift + Route safety + Access issues + Pay

Video Title: Come To WORK With Me 📦 | Amazon MORNING SHIFT
transcript:
Hi everyone. As you saw in the title, you’re coming along with me to work a 10-hour shift at Amazon. It’s 8:38 p.m. right now, and before filming I took a two-hour nap. It’s okay to rest—your body needs it so you feel prepared and motivated.
I work at an Amazon fulfillment center. In my role, we sort boxes using a scanner. In some areas, you don’t scan—you take boxes from a lane and place them into a larger box. There are different positions throughout the warehouse: non-con, shoe sort, “under the mezz,” container loading, and container building. The tasks are similar, but not exactly the same, so if you feel bored you can move to another position.
I’ve seen people say Amazon was the worst job of their life and that they overwork you. I think every warehouse is different depending on management and your position, but at my warehouse it’s actually pretty chill. One reason I like the job is that if I really don’t want to work, sometimes they offer VTO (voluntary time off). For example, if I don’t want to work my 4:30 a.m. shift, I can check for VTO, accept it, and take the day off (you just don’t get paid for that day). If you want extra cash, you can pick up shifts. Sometimes there’s a $3 premium shift—so if you normally make $18/hour, you can earn $21/hour.
My regular schedule is 4:30 a.m. to 3:00 p.m. Today I’m packing my lunch and getting ready for work.
Starting the shift:
 I wake up around 3:00 a.m., get ready quickly, grab my lunch, snacks, and head to work. There’s a locker room for employees (first come, first served). I put my stuff away and start the day.
At Amazon there are different roles. Today I was assigned to “under the mezz.” That means taking smaller boxes and putting them into a bigger box called a “shuttle.” That’s basically what I do for the shift: take Amazon packages and load them into the shuttle. We also wrap the shuttle so the smaller boxes don’t fall out—if boxes fall, it takes a lot of patience to fix.
Breaks and pacing:
 I take quick moments to stretch and drink water. The break room is big and there are vending machines. We have a 15-minute break, and I sometimes go to my car for quiet because it’s noisy inside. Later there’s a 30-minute lunch break, and then another 15-minute break later in the shift.
If you’re confused about how roles work, there’s a learning ambassador (trainer) who trains you for about three days or a week depending on your role.
Overall, I think Amazon can be a decent place to work, especially part-time for extra cash, but it depends on the person. A 10-hour shift isn’t easy, but I did it because the $3 premium pay made it worth it for me.
Topic: 10-hour shift routine + Roles/rotation + VTO/extra pay + Breaks

Video Title: 1 month at an Amazon Warehouse… (First Day experience included)
transcript:
Today is officially my first day working at the Amazon warehouse. I’ve been there about a month (a month and a half, but I’ll say a month to keep it simple), and I want to share what you should know.
First day / orientation:
 On the first day, everyone gathers at the front of the building and they direct you into a classroom. You sit there for most of the shift while they give a presentation: where everything is in the facility, the guidelines, and rules. You’re not allowed to film inside the facility—if you’re caught recording, you can lose your job.
They give you a few things during orientation, like a clear bag to carry your items. Every time you leave the facility, you go through a security checkpoint with metal detectors to make sure you’re not stealing anything or bringing in prohibited items.
After orientation, the second day is when you actually go out to the floor and into your department.
Shifts and scheduling:
 Most workers do 10-hour shifts. Amazon also has a flex schedule where you can create your own schedule. There are different flex options (for example, one that requires only a few hours a week and another that requires around 30 hours/week to keep your job). With flex, you can choose morning, mid-day, or night shifts.
Right now I’m on a “donut schedule”: Monday and Tuesday on, Wednesday off, Thursday and Friday on, and weekends off.
Department and work (Ship Dock):
 I work in Ship Dock. Packages drop into carts and either I move the carts onto the floor to be loaded onto trucks, or I move carts from the floor onto the trucks. The work is simple but involves a lot of walking. When I tracked it, I walked around 30,000 steps a day. It kills your feet, so invest in good insoles (like Dr. Scholl’s) or comfortable shoes.
In Ship Dock you need steel-toe safety shoes. Amazon gives you a credit to get them for free, so use it.
Pay and getting paid:
 Pay varies by state. In Virginia, I make about $19/hour now. Before, it was about $18.60/hour. We get paid every Wednesday. When you first start, you may have to wait two weeks for your first paycheck. They do that so people don’t work one week and quit immediately.
Amazon isn’t for the weak. The work itself isn’t always bad, but the hours are long. If you can handle long shifts mentally, you’ll be okay.
VTO (Voluntary Time Off):
 Every so often they offer VTO, which lets you drop your shift with no consequences—you just don’t get paid that day. If you see VTO and want it, you have to take it quickly because someone else will grab it. If you really need money, don’t take it. But if you want a “get out of jail” card, VTO is great.
They offer VTO because sometimes there are too many workers in the building and not enough work. So they send some people home to balance labor costs.
PTO and vacation:
 For regular workers, you earn PTO. The earn rate is about 55 minutes per 10-hour shift. So if you use one hour of PTO, that’s basically a whole day of work you’re sacrificing. Flex workers may not get PTO because they can choose their schedule.
For vacation time, you usually have to request it about two weeks in advance. You may need PTO to cover it, or you can shift-swap. If you shift-swap, someone has to pick up your shift—if nobody picks it up, you might be stuck. But usually someone picks it up quickly because there are a lot of people at the facility.
Overnight work and fatigue:
 For me, the hardest part is the hours. Working overnight isn’t hard because of the start time—it’s getting off at 4:30 a.m., sometimes 5:00 a.m. with overtime. You get home when everyone is asleep, and you really only enjoy your off days. Around the 6–7 hour mark, you’re ready to leave, but leaving early costs a lot of PTO.
Breaks:
 You get two 30-minute breaks. The first is around the 10:00 mark (10 a.m. or 10 p.m.), and the second around the 2:00 mark (2 a.m. or 2 p.m.), depending on your shift. Some departments are strict about break times; others are more flexible, so ask your manager.
Thirty minutes sounds like a lot until you actually try to go somewhere. There aren’t many food places close to my facility. If you leave to get food and come back, you can easily go over break time. I recommend bringing your own food or buying from the break room occasionally—buying all the time adds up.
Managers / rules:
 Managers vary by department and building. Most of mine are chill, but some are strict about rules like no earbuds and no phone use. Some managers might be relaxed and say, “Just don’t do it around the strict managers and make sure the work gets done.”
Some people say managers are strict about bathroom breaks. For me, it’s not like that, but it depends on your department and building.
Overall recommendation:
 Would I recommend Amazon? It depends. The pay is good, and the work itself isn’t always bad, but the long 10-hour shifts are tough mentally. I wouldn’t do it long-term, but you can build a career there if you’re committed to moving up.
Also, you may have to work certain holidays like Thanksgiving, Christmas, and New Year’s.
Topic: First month overview + Schedules + Pay + PTO/VTO + Breaks + Managers

Video Title: Amazon Workers Are MASS QUITTING After Insane Working Conditions And Low Pay
transcript:
I’m quitting. I’m done.
To my fellow courier drivers—how do you do this for a year, two years, five years? This job is exhausting. My body hurts. Does it ever get easier? The workload doesn’t change.
I keep seeing videos of people quitting Amazon, especially drivers, but also warehouse workers. I’m not saying everyone is quitting, but a lot of people leave after they realize the job isn’t what they expected. People hear “$20 an hour,” get excited, and then get there and find out it’s very labor-intensive with high expectations. Productivity is the priority.
One person in the clips said he quit on the first day because the work was too intense and he felt the job was far more demanding than expected.
In general, this is a physically demanding job. You can’t go in expecting easy money. You’re using your hands, legs, and body—moving boxes for hours. You’ll be sore after work. Whether it’s worth it depends on where you are in life. If you need the money to support your family, you make it work. If it’s just a side job and you don’t need it, you might walk away.
Another worker described his first day delivering: they gave him a truck packed full of packages and expected him to finish the route in about 4.5–5 hours. He said he had a stop where 100 packages went to one building, and he had to place each package individually into a locker. Even then, he was told he was taking too long. He mentioned delivering in downtown Chicago where traffic and crowds slow everything down. He said taking a 30-minute break could make you late on your route.
He also complained about strict driving/route violations—saying small mistakes like missing a stop sign or timing a yellow light could lead to suspension.
A driver also raised a safety concern: dog bites. He said he had been bitten twice, and described other drivers being bitten as well. He urged customers to secure their dogs when drivers are delivering.
Overall, the message is that working conditions can be intense—high physical demands, tight time expectations, and safety risks—leading some drivers and workers to quit quickly.
Topic: Driver workload + Productivity pressure + Route constraints + Safety (dogs)

Video Title: What It’s Really Like to Work at Amazon: A Real Employee Review
transcript:
Imagine a giant Amazon warehouse: shelves taller than a person, conveyors buzzing, boxes moving nonstop, workers in vests moving everywhere. In the middle of all that—me—one of thousands of employees, a small cog in a massive machine.
When I first got hired, it sounded great: stable job, solid pay, health insurance. They advertised around $18 to $22 an hour, and with overtime you could get close to $30 an hour. That’s roughly $3,500 to $4,000 a month, about $3,000 take-home after taxes. For someone without a college degree, it sounded like a dream. But the reality was different.
Day one / operations and tracking:
 On day one, you go through security—metal detectors, and you hand over your phone. The warehouse feels like a small city. My job was packing: boxes, tape, labels. The conveyor never stops, and you can’t slow down either. The quota is about 120 to 150 packages per hour. If you fall behind, the system marks you as “time off task.” Too many marks and you’re meeting with your manager, and you could get fired.
Shifts, physical load, and breaks:
 Shifts are 10 to 12 hours—four days on, three days off. That sounds okay, but after 12 hours on your feet, your days off feel like recovery days. Sometimes they call you for overtime. The pay jumps (around $27 to $32/hour), but after those shifts your legs feel like you ran a marathon.
The physical demand is heavy. You walk about 15 to 20 kilometers per shift—always moving. The scanner tracks every scan, every second. Even sitting down briefly becomes a problem. Breaks are short: two 15-minute breaks and one 30-minute lunch. And sometimes it takes five minutes just to walk to the break room, so a “15-minute break” can feel like seven minutes.
Pay and tradeoffs:
 The pay is good. I started at $21.50/hour. Night shifts paid an extra dollar, and overtime was time-and-a-half. That added up to around $3,800 to $4,000 a month before taxes, about $3,000 net. Sometimes there were small bonuses or discounts. Compared to places like Walmart or Target, Amazon paid better.
Working conditions and burnout:
 The downsides are real: constant soreness in your legs and back, and constant monitoring—cameras, scanners, sensors tracking your movements. Sometimes it felt like robots would have it easier than humans. In summer it’s too hot; in winter it’s too cold. People break down on shift. I’ve seen co-workers cry or walk out and never return.
Upsides and career path:
 There are upsides too: pay is on time, extra hours can mean serious money, and you get benefits like health insurance and stock. There are chances to move up—team lead or forklift operator roles can pay around $25 to $28/hour. If you can handle it, there’s a path forward.
Bottom line:
 Amazon isn’t heaven and it isn’t hell. It’s a conveyor belt. If you want money, you grind and endure. If you don’t, you leave. I lasted over a year. It’s good if you need to save up or stay afloat, but long-term it can burn you out fast. Customers see fast delivery, but they don’t see the people who spent 12 hours running across the warehouse to make it happen.
Topic: Warehouse reality check + Quotas/monitoring + Physical strain + Pay/benefits

Video Title: Chilling working at am Amazon Fulfillment Center transportation associate
transcript:
What’s up, YouTube? I’m going to show you a bit of what I do as a hostler driver in Amazon Transportation.
First, you inspect the trailer. After you inspect it, you come back, grab your “Tommy” phone, and document that there are no issues. If there is an issue, you enter what’s wrong in the system.
You put on gloves as part of PPE.
Parking policy and methods:
 There are only two ways we’re allowed to park a trailer at a dock door or parking slip. Amazon policy says we’re not allowed to “alley dock.” So we either do an S-turn or a U/J-turn.
An S-turn is when you pull up on the same side as the parking slip or dock door, go up about two slips or two dock doors, then crank the wheel all the way right, then all the way left, so you end up at an angle and can back in. Everyone has their own technique.
The benchmark is about 10 minutes. I can do it in five sometimes, but I stopped rushing. The faster I go, the more I end up doing other people’s work, and I got tired of that.
Trailer seal and procedure:
 When you get to the trailer, you cut the seal, open the doors, and park the trailer. You don’t throw the seal away or leave it on the ground—you put it in a bucket (an RFID bucket). To open, you open the passenger-side door first, then the driver-side. To close, you close the driver-side first, then the passenger-side.
Working conditions (heat / equipment):
 It gets really hot out here. The AC in these hostler vehicles isn’t great, and the small fan on top doesn’t work well. When it’s hot, I’m not going as fast anymore—I used to do five-minute moves, but not in this heat. I sweat a lot, so I slow down and try to get inside where there’s AC when I can. Hydration matters too.
Pay notes:
 I’m in California. Starting pay is around the mid-$20s per hour, and pay can increase every six months. But California is expensive, so that matters.
Topic: Yard/hostler job duties + Safety/PPE + Parking maneuvers + Pay progression
